coredata.dc:title: Visible light-activated self-powered photoelectrochemical aptasensor for ultrasensitive chloramphenicol detection based on DFT-proved Z-scheme Ag2CrO4/g-C3N4/graphene oxide
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Bo
Lu, Yue
Luo, Jun
Zhang, Ziling
Zhu, Xu
Tang, Lin
Wang, Lingling
Deng, Yaocheng
Ouyang, Xilian
Tan, Jisui
Wang, Jiajia
coredata.dc:description: A visible light self-powered photoelectrochemical (PEC) aptasensor based on silver chromate particles, graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets and graphene oxide sheets (Ag2CrO4/g-C3N4/GO) for the ultrasensitive detection of chloramphenicol (CAP) was reported in this work. g-C3N4 was considered to be the fundamental photoelectric material because of its great oxidation ability of photogenerated hole as well as excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity. However, the narrow light absorption range and rapid carrier recombination rate limit the application of pure g-C3N4. Herein, Ag2CrO4 and GO as photosensitizer were introduced to improve the photoelectric properties of g-C3N4. The photocurrent of the developed ternary composite was about 3 times higher than that of pristine g-C3N4, which proves it can be used as a suitable photoelectric active material. Moreover, the mechanism of Z-scheme electron transfer path was proved by density functional theory (DFT) calculation. The fabricated PEC aptasensor exhibited high sensitivity toward CAP with a wide liner response of 0.5 pM to 50 nM and a detection limit of 0.29 pM. The specific recognition mechanism and excellent sensing performance indicated this aptasensor could serve as a useful tool for selective and ultrasensitive CAP detection in practical analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Z-scheme
Self-powered
Density functional theory
Photoelectrochemical aptasensor
Chloramphenicol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Pristine g-C3N4 nanosheets, GO sheets and Ag2CrO4 particles
Characterization of the as fabricated PEC aptasensor
Optimization of experimental conditions for PEC detection
The proof of the proposed detection mechanism
Application of the aptasensor in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CGA-30 (A); HR-TEM images of CGA-30 (B); The EDS analysis of CGA-30 (C); FT-IR spectrum (D) of of (a) GO, (b) AO, (c) CG-1, (d) CN, (e) CGA-30; XRD patterns of AO, CGA-30, CGA-30-200CGA-30-200 °C, CG-1, CN (E); XPS survey spectrum of CGA-30 (F).
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (A), the band gap energy (B) and photoluminescence spectra (C) of AO, CGA-30, CG-1 and CN.

Construction the process of the proposed sensor for CAP detection (A); Electrochemical impedance spectra of different electrodes (B): (a) FTO, (b) CN/FTO, (c) CG-1/FTO, (d) CGA-30/FTO, (e) CA/FTO, (f) GA/FTO (g) GLD/CGA-30/FTO and (h) aptamer/GLD/CGA-30/FTO, which using phosphate buffer (pH = 7.4) containing 5 mM ferro/ferricyanide and 20 mM KCl, with frequency range of 0.1-105 Hz; Photocurrent responses of different modified FTO electrodes recorded in 0.1 M Na2SO4 (C): (a) CGA-30/FTO, (b) GLD/CGA-30/FTO, (c) aptamer/GLD/CGA-30/FTO, (d) CG-1/FTO, (e) aptamer/GLD/CGA-30/FTO incubated with 20 nM CAP, (f) CN/FTO, (g) FTO, (h) CA/FTO, (i) GA/FTO.

Photocurrent responses of the developed sensor incubated with different concentrations of CAP: from a to h 5 × 10−4, 2 × 10−3, 0.02, 0.1, 0.5, 3.1, 20 and 150 nM (A); Calibration curve for CAP on the PEC aptasensor (B); The photocurrent responses determined on PEC aptasensor incubated with 20 nM CAP solution containing 20 nM different antibiotics (C); The photocurrent responses of the five independent photoelectrochemical biosensors (D); The stability of aptamer/GLD/CGA-30/FTO on the photocurrent response of PEC aptasensor toward 20 nM CAP (E).

Three dimensional (3D) charge difference density plot of the interfaces of CGA heterostructure where brown, silver, gray, jacinth, and blue represent C, N, Ag, O, and Cr atoms, respectively, green and red indicate the consumption and accumulation of charge, respectively (A); The planar averaged charge density difference for CGA heterostructure (B); The charge transfer process mechanism of CGA heterostructure under visible-light irradiation (C).

head.ce:title: Visible light-activated self-powered photoelectrochemical aptasensor for ultrasensitive chloramphenicol detection based on DFT-proved Z-scheme Ag2CrO4/g-C3N4/graphene oxide

Hunan University and Key Laboratory of Environmental Biology and Pollution Control (Hunan University)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Resources and Environment, Hunan Agricultural University, Changsha, 410128, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Resources and Environment, Hunan Agricultural University, Changsha 410128, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A visible light self-powered photoelectrochemical (PEC) aptasensor based on silver chromate particles, graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets and graphene oxide sheets (Ag2CrO4/g-C3N4/GO) for the ultrasensitive detection of chloramphenicol (CAP) was reported in this work. g-C3N4 was considered to be the fundamental photoelectric material because of its great oxidation ability of photogenerated hole as well as excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity. However, the narrow light absorption range and rapid carrier recombination rate limit the application of pure g-C3N4. Herein, Ag2CrO4 and GO as photosensitizer were introduced to improve the photoelectric properties of g-C3N4. The photocurrent of the developed ternary composite was about 3 times higher than that of pristine g-C3N4, which proves it can be used as a suitable photoelectric active material. Moreover, the mechanism of Z-scheme electron transfer path was proved by density functional theory (DFT) calculation. The fabricated PEC aptasensor exhibited high sensitivity toward CAP with a wide liner response of 0.5 pM to 50 nM and a detection limit of 0.29 pM. The specific recognition mechanism and excellent sensing performance indicated this aptasensor could serve as a useful tool for selective and ultrasensitive CAP detection in practical analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: As a newly promising detection technique for biomolecules and chemical substances analysis, photoelectrochemical (PEC) aptasensor has attracted considerable attention due to its remarkable sensitivity, low background, high selectivity, easy miniaturization and low cost (Wang et al., 2009; Zang et al., 2018). Recently, new types of self-powered PEC biosensors have demonstrated the ability to perform detection without the demand for external power sources (Cakiroglu and Ozacar, 2018; Dong et al., 2020; Du et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020b). In PEC sensing, photoelectric active materials play a vital role since they undertake the photoelectric conversion task. Separation enhancement and recombination reduction of carriers are the focuses of improving materials performance. Therefore, various methods have been applied to improve the above factors, one of which is to replace the common type II heterojunction with the Z-scheme heterojunction (Jiang et al., 2020b; Xu et al., 2018b; Yan et al., 2019; You et al., 2019). However, a majority of photoelectric active materials, such as metal oxides like TiO2 (Shi et al., 2018), metal sulfides like Ag2S (Zhang et al., 2020a), transition metal dichalcogenides like WS2 (Sui et al., 2019), are limited by their wide band gap or narrow light absorption ability, which results in low PEC response and weak absorption of visible light. Using nanomaterials and building heterojunction are effective and simple ways to overcome the shortcomings (Wang et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020). Hence, it is essential to seek and synthesize suitable nanomaterials with appropriate PEC properties, excellent biocompatibility, high stability and strong visible light absorption to realize ideal PEC detection performance.

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), as a novel metal-free 2D semiconductor material, has attracted public attentions recently. Due to its characteristics of unique optical and electrical properties, layered structure, high chemical and thermal stability, excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity, g-C3N4 has been widely used in photocatalysis (Deng et al., 2018; Jiang et al., 2020a; Tang et al., 2018), bioimaging (Oh et al., 2015; Zhan et al., 2017), electrocatalysis (Han et al., 2019; Zheng et al., 2017) and biosensing (Fang et al., 2016; Peng et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2019). However, the pure g-C3N4 has some limits such as high recombination rate of photogenerated carriers, narrow light absorption range and low specific surface areas. Accordingly, there are several approaches emerged to overcome the drawbacks, including doping photoactive elements, copolymerizing with electronenriched molecules, adjusting the shape to form small-size nanostructure and establishing heterojunction with proper materials (Duan et al., 2019; Fu et al., 2017; Jun et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2014). Among all, establishing heterojunction is a facile and efficient way to improve the performance of g-C3N4. Motaghed Mazhabi et al. designed a label-free PEC aptamer-based on g-C3N4/AgI nanocomposite for the specific detection of cancer cells, which significantly enhanced the photocurrent response of pure g-C3N4 (Motaghed Mazhabi et al., 2018). Wang and coworkers proposed TiO2/g-C3N4 as the photoelectric active material, which realized the ultrasensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase activity (Wang et al., 2018a). Lately, our group successfully fabricated an atomic scale g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 heterojunction (Wang et al., 2017) and a Z-scheme CoO/Au/g-C3N4 heterojunction (Tang et al., 2019b), which exhibited superior capability for degradation or detection of pollutants. In many works, g-C3N4 is often used to construct Z-scheme heterojunction with other materials, which exhibited excellent performance (Ye et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2017). These results indicated the feasibility of using heterojunction to improve the performance of g-C3N4, but still, further improvements on PEC performance of g-C3N4 are required to meet the increasing requirements of substances tests and practical applications.

Recently, silver-based compounds have become popular materials owing to their proper morphology and structure with high electron transfer capability and visible light utilization rate, which makes them become promising materials in PEC platform construction (Fan et al., 2019; Tang et al., 2019a; Zhang et al., 2019). Among various silver-based compounds, silver chromate (Ag2CrO4) has been widely used because of its unique crystal structure and electronic property with a narrow band gap, which may favor the improvement of g-C3N4 (Feizpoor et al., 2017; Jalali et al., 2019; Ye et al., 2018). As reported, Ag2CrO4/g-C3N4 composites exhibited better photocatalytic activity and stability toward the degradation of organic pollutants than either monomers (Shang et al., 2017). Besides, Ag2CrO4 and g-C3N4 can form a Z-scheme heterojunction, which further broadened the light absorption region, enhanced charge separation rate and strengthened redox ability compared to traditional heterostructures (Luo et al., 2016). Our team took advantage of the Z-scheme to produce a Ag2CrO4/g-C3N4 photocatalyst, which presented better performance in the degradation of the dye contaminants (Deng et al., 2016). Nonetheless, Ag2CrO4 has a troublesome problem of photocorrosion that may have an adverse effect on its performance. Therefore, it is crucial to find a way to achieve higher stability and activity. Ag2CrO4 modified with graphene oxide (GO) was reported to well improve the photocatalytic properties (Xu et al., 2015). Mao et al. had certified that GO could be regarded as an ideal electron mediator for electrons transmission during the PEC reaction, hence minimizing the recombination rate of the electron-hole pair (Mao et al., 2019). Therefore, the idea of using Ag2CrO4/g-C3N4/GO composite as the photoelectric active material was determined.

Chloramphenicol (CAP), a broad-spectrum antibiotic substance, has been widely used in the treatment and prevention of diseases for human and livestock breeding industry, owing to its cheap and effective antibacterial effect (Lofrano et al., 2016; Munawar et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2015). Nowadays, CAP is often found in rivers and solid wastes due to livestock waste emission, while CAP residues have also been found in animal-derived foods such as milk, meat and honey. Related researches reported that CAP has serious toxic and side effects on the human body, so most of the countries have restricted or banned the application of CAP in animal-derived food, and have carried out strict control on clinical usage (Hanekamp and Bast, 2015). However, most detection methods like chemiluminescence (Thongchai et al., 2010), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Kawano et al., 2015) and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (Bhowmik et al., 2017) are limited due to their high cost or time-consuming, therefore, it is urgent to develop highly sensitive and selective CAP analysis technologies.

Thus, we developed a PEC aptasensor for CAP detection based on ternary Ag2CrO4/g-C3N4/GO nanocomposite with the combination of the specific aptamer. In the proposed PEC aptasensor, the aptamer was used to quickly and accurately identify and capture CAP. Notably, the efficient Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism was substantiated by density functional theory (DFT) calculation, which is seldom lucubrated in prior studies. Benefiting from the good photoelectric properties of the designed composite and the unique electron transfer route, the as-fabricated PEC aptasensor presented good performance with rapid response, wide linear detection range, low detection limit. Furthermore, the specific recognition between target CAP molecules and aptamer guaranteed the high selectivity and good practical application of the sensor.

Chloramphenicol (CAP), kanamycin, oxytetracycline, doxycycline, chlorotetracycline, ciprofloxacin, neomycin sulfate and tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Nafion (5 wt%) was obtained from Rhawn Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other reagents and materials of analytical grade were obtained commercially from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. and used as received without further purification. Ultra-pure water (18.25 MΩ cm−1) from a Milli-pore Q water purification system was used in all experiments. The amino-functionalized CAP aptamer with sequences of 5′-NH2-(CH2)6-ACTTCAGTGAGTTGTCCCACGGTCGGCGAGTCGGTGGTAG-3′ was synthesized by Shanghai Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

All PEC and electrochemical measurements were performed using a CHI 660D workstation in a three-electrode PEC cell, in which a bare/modified fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) electrode was used as working electrode, a Pt electrode as the counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. A 300 W Xe lamp (PLS/SXE 300C, Beijing Perfectlight Co., Ltd.) was used as the visible light source with the wavelength of 420 nm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were carried on a Bruker AXS D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu-Kα irradiation source. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEOL JEM-3010 (JEOL, Japan). FT-IR spectra was operated on an IRAffinity-1 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was obtained on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi to analyze chemical compositions of samples. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra was operated on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer. Ultraviolet visible diffused reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) was measured on a Cary 300 UV–vis spectrophotometer (USA) equipped with an integrating sphere. The electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis were examined on a Bruker ER200-SRC spectrometer under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pristine g-C3N4 nanosheets, GO sheets and Ag2CrO4 particles
ce:section.ce:para: Pristine g-C3N4 nanosheets were prepared by a modified heat-etching strategy using melamine as the precursor according to the previous literatures (Deng et al., 2016, 2018). Briefly, melamine was heated at 500 °C for 2 h at a rate of 2 °C/min, and followed by heating at 520 °C for another 2 h at the same heating rate. The obtained bulk g-C3N4 was milled into powder in a mortar and heated at 520 °C for 4 h at a heating rate of 2 °C/min. The obtained light yellow powder was g-C3N4 nanosheets and labeled as CN. GO sheets were synthesized by a modified Hummer’s method through the oxidation of graphite powers (Pang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018b). Pristine Ag2CrO4 particles were prepared by a simple precipitation method. In brief, 100 mL of 0.03 mol L−1 K2CrO4 solution was dripped into 100 mL of 0.06 mol L−1 AgNO3 solution with strong stirring for 1 h. The precipitate was obtained by filtration and washed with ethanol and water several times, then dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven for 8 h. The pristine Ag2CrO4 particles were labeled as AO.

The ternary Ag2CrO4/CN/GO composites were fabricated by a self-assembly precipitation method, which was labeled as CGA. Briefly, the prepared GO (0.5 mg) and CN (50 mg) were dispersed in 100 mL ultra-pure water and then ultra-sonicated for 12 h. Then a certain volume of AgNO3 solution (0.06 mol/L) was slowly dripped into the above mixtures and stirred for 12 h to make Ag+ well adsorbed or dispersed on the surface of GO and CN under dark condition. Subsequently, the same amount of K2CrO4 solution (0.03 mol/L) was dripped into the obtained Ag+-CN/GO suspension. After 4 h stirring, the CGA composites was collected by filtration and washed with ultra-pure water for several times and dried at 60 °C in vacuum overnight. The CGA composite with different theoretical weight ratios of Ag2CrO4 were marked as CGA-5, CGA-10, CGA-30, CGA-50 and CGA-70 when the mass ratios of Ag2CrO4 to g-C3N4 are 5 %, 10 %, 30 %, 50 % and 70 %, respectively. For comparison, binary CN/GO composite were also synthesized under the same condition without the addition of AgNO3 and K2CrO4 solution. According to the reported literatures that the optimal concentration of GO to CN was 1 wt% (Miao et al., 2017; Xiang et al., 2011), the different amount of GO to CN (i.e. 0.5 %, 1 %, 3 % and 5 %) was also studied in this work. The obtained composites were marked as CG-X, where ‘X’ showed the mass ratio of GO to CN. The binary Ag2CrO4/CN (marked as CA) and CN/GO (marked as CG) composites were obtained under the same conditions without adding GO and Ag2CrO4, respectively.

Before modification, the FTO (1.0 cm × 2.0 cm) was ultrasonically cleaned in acetone, ethanol and ultra-pure water for 20 min, respectively, and then dried with nitrogen gas. Next, 100 μL of CGA-30 suspension (4 mg mL−1, dispersed in Nafion/ethanol solution (v/v, 1:9)) was coated on the cleaned FTO surface to form a film. To enhance the stability, the film was calcinated at 200 °C for 10 min in a muffle oven and naturally cooling to room temperature. The obtained modified electrode was marked as CGA-30/FTO. Then, a 20 μL of glutaraldehyde (GLD) aqueous solution (0.25 %, v/w) used as cross-linking was modified onto the CGA-30/FTO surface, and remained in dark for 1 h at room temperature, followed by rinsing with water and being dried under nitrogen gas. The obtained electrode was labelled as GLD/CGA-30/FTO. Subsequently, 20 μL of 2 μM CAP aptamer (dissolved in Tris-EDTA buffer (TE, pH 8.0)) was dropped onto the GLD/CGA-30/FTO surface and remained at 4 °C for 12 h, labelled as the aptamer/GLD/CGA-30/FTO. The modified electrode was rinsed with ultra-pure water to remove unbounded aptamers and dried with nitrogen gas. When not in use, the obtained electrodes were stored in a moist state at 4 °C.

The fabricated aptamer/GLD/CGA-30/FTO electrode was immersed into a 0.01 M phosphate buffer solution containing CAP of different concentrations at 60 °C for 1 h. After being washed with ultra-pure water, the resulting electrode was measured in a PEC cell containing 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution to record the photocurrent responses. All the measurements were performed at room temperature.

Our calculations were performed by using the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP)1 with the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE)2 exchange-correlation functional. The cutoff energy for the plane-wave basis set was 450 eV, and a 2 × 1×1 k-point gamma-centered mesh was used for the calculation. The forces less than 0.05 eV/Å were the ultimate goal of the optimization of all atomic positions. The total energy of the criterion was 10−6 eV. A vacuum space of 15 Å along the z direction was used to separate the interactions between neighboring slabs. The IDIPOL tag was set to three to switch on dipole corrections to the total energy along the z direction (Kresse and Furthmüller, 1996; Perdew et al., 1996).

Prior to analysis, the pig urine and river water removed suspended substances by filtering through a 0.45 μm membrane respectively. Before PEC testing, the sample needs to neutralize the pH around 7.0 with PBS. For the HPLC detection preprocessing, filtrate added with moderate HCl to adjust the pH to about 3.0 following the solid-phase extraction. Meanwhile the waters oasis HLB was appropriately rinsed by pure water and a mixture of methanol and water (95:5, v/v) in turn. After vacuum evacuation nearly 5 min, the pretreated sample was eluted by methanol/ethyl acetate solution (10:90, v/v), and then dried with nitrogen gas at 40 °C. Finally, the desiccated sample was dissolved in 1 mL standard solution and repeated the above filtering procedure.

The morphologies of the developed material were investigated by TEM. From Fig. 1 A, it can be seen that GO and CN possess clear sheet structures with a smooth surface. Compared to CN, GO is thinner and more transparent. Moreover, a large amount of darker Ag2CrO4 nanoparticles with diameters in the range of 5−10 nm are well-distributed on the surface of GO and g-C3N4. As shown in the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1B), the marked lattice spacing of 0.276 nm is attributed to the (002) plane of Ag2CrO4. Additionally, GO exhibits specific crystalline nature of graphene clearly, while CN has an amorphous structure. Furthermore, energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) was used to study the chemical compositions of the as-prepared samples. Fig. 1C reveals the existence of C, N, O, Cr and Ag elements in CGA-30. These results proved that GO and CN could provide high surface area for Ag2CrO4 nanoparticles growth, and CGA was successfully synthesized, which would be beneficial for fast photogenerated carriers transfer.

The chemical structure of the GO, AO, CG-1, CN, CGA-30 samples were further studied by the FTIR spectra. As shown in Fig. 1D, the broad absorption regions at 3000−3300 cm−1 and 1200−1600 cm−1 were attributed to the NH or OH stretching vibration and the typical CN heterocycles, respectively. The strong peak at 810 cm−1 was contributed by the triazine units vibrations. For the pure AO, the strong absorption peaks at 884 cm−1 was associated with CrO stretching vibrations of CrO4 2− groups, which can be found in the CGA-30 composite confirming the existence of Ag2CrO4. For the pure GO, some peaks at 1049 cm−1, 1223 cm−1, 1406 cm−1, 1620 cm−1 and 1726 cm−1 were attributed to alkoxy CO, epoxy COC, tertiary COH, aromatic CC and carboxyl CO
, respectively. However, these specific peaks of GO were not observed in CG-1 and CGA-30 composites due to the high overlap of CN. To further investigate the crystal structure of the prepared materials, the XRD patterns were obtained and shown in Fig. 1E. The CN sample exhibited 2 peaks at 13° and 27°, corresponding to the (100) and (002) planes, respectively (JCPDS No. 87-1526). Peaks appearing in the pristine AO sample could be well-indexed to the orthorhombic structure of Ag2CrO4 (JCPDS No. 26-0952). After the introduction of Ag2CrO4, both of diffraction peaks of g-C3N4 and Ag2CrO4 could be observed in the patterns of CGA-30 samples, confirming the hybrid composites were successfully synthesized. However, there were not obvious diffraction peaks of GO exhibiting in the patterns of CGA-30 and CG-1 samples since the amount of GO was too low. What’s more, in order to investigate whether the heat treatment affect the composition and structure of the CGA powder, XRD was used to study the powder collected after calcinated at 200 °C for 10 min in a muffle oven. As shown in Fig. 1, all of diffraction peaks of g-C3N4 and Ag2CrO4 could also be observed in the patterns of the calcined powder (CGA-30-200 °C), confirming the heat treatment couldn’t affect the composition and structure of the proposed samples.

XPS characterization was used to investigate the surface chemical compositions of the as-prepared samples. As shown in Fig. 1F, the survey XPS spectrum confirmed the coexistence of C, N, O, Cr and Ag in the sample of CGA-30. High-resolution XPS spectra for C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, Cr 2p and Ag 3d of CGA-30 were illustrated in Fig. S1. In Fig. S1A, two C 1s peaks at 284.6 and 288.1 eV were ascribed to CC and CN species, respectively. In the N 1s spectrum (Fig. S1B), it can be separated into four peaks located at about 398.6, 399.8, 401.2 and 404.9 eV. These were contributed to CNC, N(C)
3, side NH groups and CNH
, respectively. The O 1s spectrum in Fig. S1C could be fitted into two peaks with binding energies of 529.5 and 531.3 eV, ascribable to the lattice oxygen of pristine Ag2CrO4 and the water species or external OH group. Besides, the Cr 2p peaks at 578.5 and 587.7 eV correspond to the Cr 2p3/2 and Cr 2p1/2, respectively, while the other strong peak at 572.4 eV was assigned to Ag 3p3/2. Additionally, the peaks at binding energy values of 367.8 and 373.6 eV for Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 respectively were observed in the Ag 3d spectrum. Therefore, it can be concluded that the Ag2CrO4 nanoparticles were successfully modified on the surface of the GO and CN, which qualitatively agrees with TEM and XRD results.

To investigate the optical absorbance property of the samples, UV–vis DRS measurements were conducted (Fig. 2 A). It can be found that the absorption edges of pure CN and AO are about 450 and 720 nm, respectively. After the introduction of GO, the light absorption intensity of CG-1 was lightly strengthened due to the photosensitization GO, which is beneficial for the PEC activity improvement. Moreover, the as-prepared CGA-30 exhibited an enhanced absorption in the visible region implying an excellent transfer route of photogenerated carriers among AO, CN and GO. According to the intercept of the tangents to the plots of (αhν)1/2 vs. photon energy, the band gap of the as-synthesized samples were calculated and showed in Fig. 2B. It can be seen that the band gaps of pure CN and AO are about 2.78 and 1.77 eV, respectively. Although GO was added into the CN, the band gaps of CG-1 is the same as CN because GO is considered as a kind of zero gap semiconductor. However, the band gap of CGA-30 is calculated to be 2.72 eV, which is contributed to the different band gap energies of bare CN and AO. The narrow band gap could be beneficial for the improvement of visible light absorption.

The PL measurement was employed to investigate the migration, transfer and recombination processes of photo-induced charge carriers in the as-prepared semiconductors. As shown in Fig. 2C, the emission peaks of all the samples center around 450 nm followed the order of CN > CG-1 > CGA-30 > AO. The CGA-30 sample possesses the lowest emission intensity when compare to bare CN and CG-1, suggesting a lower photogenerated carrier recombination. The result can be assigned to high conductivity of GO and narrow band gap of AO, which can significantly improve the efficient charge separation and transfer in the heterojunction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the as fabricated PEC aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the the actual and optimal composition of the samples, the photocurrent response tests over several on-off cycles under visible light irradiation of CG-x and CGA-x was shown in Fig. S2. Compared with pure CN, the photocurrent generated by CG-X increased first and then reduced with the increase of x value, and the strongest photocurrent response was obtained at CG-1 (Fig. S2A). Similarly, when AO was introduced into CG, the photocurrent responses of ternary composites were obvious higher than that of CG. As shown in Fig. S2B, CGA-30 possessed the highest photocurrent response intensity. Hence, we selected 1 wt% GO and 30 wt% Ag2CrO4 to synthesize the ideal material for the fabrication of the proposed PEC biosensor.

To further support the efficient separation of photoinduced charge carriers and the conductivity of the as-prepared samples, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements and photocurrent response tests were conducted. Fig. 3 B and its inset exhibit charge transfer resistance of different modified electrodes, corresponding to the semicircle section. It can be seen that CGA-30/FTO has the smallest semicircle diameter among all the prepared electrodes except bare FTO, revealing that the separation and transfer efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers are significantly enhanced by the interfacial interaction among CN, GO and AO. After the successive modification of GLD and aptamer on the surface of CGA-30/FTO, the resistance increased markedly because of the repelling effect of the GLD and aptamer. The corresponding values of charge transfer resistance were calculated and showed in Table S1. Furthermore, the photocurrent response tests of different modified electrodes were conducted over several on-off cycles under visible light irradiation and shown in Fig. 3C. Compared with the photocurrent response of CN/FTO (curve f), CG-1/FTO (curve d), CA/FTO (curve h) and GA/FTO (curve i), the electrode modified with CGA-30 (curve a) possessed the highest photocurrent intensity, confirming the higher visible light absorption and excellent separation ability of photogenerated carriers.

After the consecutive modification of the GLD and aptamer on the surface of CGA-30/FTO, the photocurrent intensity had a certain degree of decline due to the steric hindrance of GLD and biomolecules, consist with the EIS results. Upon using the developed electrode to detect 20 nM CAP, the photocurrent response obviously increased again. It was because with the existence of aptamer, the CAP could bind to it specifically, and then the CAP molecules captured by aptamer would participate in the photocatalytic reactions (Jiang et al., 2019; Jodat and Jodat, 2013; Liu et al., 2015). According to previous researches, the CAP molecules could be synergistically oxidized by photogenerated holes, hydroxyl radical (O
H) that created by the reactions of holes with OH− or H2O, and superoxide radical (
O2 −) that created by the reaction of electrons with O2 (Chatzitakis et al., 2008; Gao et al., 2017; Qin et al., 2019). To prove the correctness of the mechanism, ESR technology was used to detect
OH and O
2 − in the CGA composite reaction system for the oxidation of CAP molecule under visible light. 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-Noxide (DMPO) was added to the system as a spin trap for stabilizing radicals. Under dark conditions, no obvious peaks of O
H were found in Fig.S3A. However, when the CGA-30 was irradiated with visible light, the characteristic peaks of O
H were detected by ESR. With the extension of the irradiation time, the signal intensity increased, indicating that plenty of photogenerated holes participated in the production of O
H radicals. Similarly, the CGA-30 curve in Fig. S3B demonstrated the presence of
O2 − in the reaction, which confirmed the CAP molecules could be oxidized by
OH and O
2 −. The above reaction inhibited the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, ultimately exhibited the increased high photocurrent. The construction process of the sensor platform is shown in Fig. 3A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions for PEC detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to get a more ideal signal response, some experimental conditions were optimized during the construction process. To compare the response quantitatively, the value of photocurrent (I) after incubation with CAP (20 nM) was evaluated.

Fig. S2A illustrates the influence of aptamer concentration on the photocurrent response of the fabricated sensor. It was observed that when the concentration of aptamer immobilized on the modified electrode was increased from 1 to 5 μM, the response of the sensor increased. This is because the high concentration allows more CAP molecules to be captured by aptamer, the amount of CAP participate in the reaction and I raise subsequently. However, with further increment, the response varied slightly, indicating that the aptamer concentration on the electrode surface has reached saturation. Therefore, the optimized concentration of aptamer was chosen as 5 μM. Meanwhile, the incubation time of aptamer is deemed to be a pivotal factor in PEC sensing, which could affect the residual aptamer volume on the electrode directly. As exhibited in Fig. S2B, the photocurrent response enhanced with changing the incubation time of aptamer from 0 to 40 min, then the response saturated as the potential exceeded 40 min. Thus, 40 min was employed.

The binding time between CAP and aptamer has great effects on the PEC response since higher binding time may facilitate superior binding efﬁciency for CAP and aptamer. Results based on the as-constructed aptasensor was illustrated in Fig. S2C, the photocurrent increased following with increasing binding time up to 60 min and then change negligibly. Similarly, the photocurrent response increased as the incubation temperature rose and then leveled off after 37 °C (Fig. S2D), which indicates the specific binding of aptamer and CAP achieved saturation. Therefore, 60 min and 37 °C were selected as optimum time and temperature for CAP binding respectively.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, the developed aptamer/CGA/FTO electrode was applied to investigate CAP quantitatively. Fig. 4 A displayed the PEC responses to different concentrations of CAP molecules. It can be seen that the photocurrent increased with the increasing CAP concentration because more CAP molecules were captured and then involved in the PEC process. The signal response of the sensor was found to have a linear relationship with CAP concentration within the limits of 0.5 pM to 50 nM. The linear equation was expressed as I/nA = 54.987lg(C/nM) + 1050.4 (R2 = 0.997) (Fig. 4B), and the detection limit was estimated to be 0.29 pM (S/N = 3). Compared with previous works for CAP determination (Table S2), the proposed method revealed remarkable performance with high sensitivity. It can not only meet the demand of detecting CAP in the biological fluids and pharmaceutical process but also be quite useful for the rapid trace CAP in food residues and environmental waters.

For evaluating the selectivity of the developed PEC aptasensor, the PEC responses toward some other antibiotics were studied (chloramphenicol, chlorotetracycline, ciprofloxacin, doxycycline, kanamycin, neomycin sulfate and oxytetracycline). As shown in Fig. 4C, negligible responses on the proposed sensor were observed with the interference of other antibiotics, illustrating the high selectivity of this sensor owing to the specific integration between target CAP molecules and aptamer. Furthermore, the reproducibility of the developed PEC sensor was proved by examining the responses of five independently prepared PEC biosensors (Fig. 4D). After calculation, the RSD value of the responses for 20 nM CAP detection was 2.1 %, suggesting the great reproducibility. In addition, Fig. 4E depicted the aptamer/GLD/CGA/FTO electrode stored at 4 ℃ in the refrigerator did not emerge obvious change in PEC response after two weeks, indicating the satisfactory stability of this sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The proof of the proposed detection mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
The valence band (EVB) edge position and the conduction band (ECB) edge position of the proposed samples were estimated as the following formulas (Li et al., 2015; Meng et al., 2015):
(1) ECB = X-Ec-1/2Eg
(2) EVB = ECB + Eg
where Ec represents the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (about 4.5 eV), X represents the electronegativity of the material, according to the previous reports, the values of the X for AO and CN were 5.83 eV and 4.72 eV, respectively (Huang et al., 2013; Xu et al., 2015). Eg is the band gap energy of the material, while Fig. 2B showed the values of the Eg for CN and AO were 2.78 eV and 1.77 eV, respectively. After calculating, it can be known that the EVB and ECB of CN were about 1.61 eV and -1.17 eV, the EVB and ECB of AO were about 2.22 eV and 0.45 eV, respectively.

If the charge transfer process of the CGA composite followed the traditional Ⅱ-scheme, the electrons would accumulate in the CB of AO for the reduction reaction and the holes would migrate to the VB of CN. However, the ECB of AO was more negative than the
O2 −/O2 potential (−0.33 V vs. NHE), it was difficult for O2 to capture the photogenerated electrons in the CB of AO to produce
O2 −. Thus, the traditional Ⅱ-scheme heterojunction nearly couldn’t promote
O2 − production. But we used electron spin resonance (ESR) technology to detect the production of
O2 − in methanol system of various materials under visible-light irradiation. As shown in Fig. S3B, the distinct characteristic peaks of DMPO-
O2 − for CN, CG-1 and CGA-30 sample could be observed, indicating that
O2 − radicals were generated on the surface of these samples after visible light irradiation. There were no obvious peaks of DMPO-
O2 − observed in the AO pattern. Furthermore, the
O2 − signal intensity of the CGA-30 composite was obviously stronger than that of other two samples, implying that the amount of
O2 − radical generated on the surface of CGA-30 composite was higher than that of CN and CG-1 under visible light irradiation. The result suggested that plenty of electrons in the CB of CN participated in the reduction reaction for
O2 − radical production. Therefore, it could be proposed that the charge transfer way may follow Z-scheme mechanism rather than traditional Ⅱ-scheme.

To lucubrate the Z-scheme charge carriers transfer process, we have explored the charge distribution of CGA heterostructure by DFT calculation. The quantitative result was displayed in Fig. 5 . Hereon Δρ is defined below
(3)
Δρ = ρCAG – ρAg2CrO4 – ρg-C3N4 - ρGO
where ρCAG , ρAg2CrO4 , and ρGO represent the charge densities of each component in the same configuration. The positive and negative values represent the accumulation and depletion of electrons, which are corresponding to the red and green area respectively in Fig. 5A. For verifying the presence of interfacial charge transfer among GO, Ag2CrO4 and g-C3N4, the 3D charge difference density plot of the interfaces of CGA heterostructure is showed in Fig. 5A. From the bottom to the top are represented by Ag2CrO4, g-C3N4 and GO. It is observed that the electrons are accumulated in the g-C3N4 firstly, while they are depleted in Ag2CrO4, indicating the interface had produced an internal electric field (IEF). Then the electrons accumulated in g-C3N4 migrate to GO, further separating electron-hole pairs. Fig. 5B depicts the planar averaged charge density difference corresponding to the CGA heterojunction. Significant changes in the charge density difference at the interface can be found on account of the IEF between GO, Ag2CrO4 and g-C3N4. Owing to the value of Δρ that indicates electron accumulation or depletion, the IEF direction can be known through calculating the variation of it. Obviously, at the CA interface, the electrons are inclined to transfer from Ag2CrO4 to g-C3N4, while they are inclined to shift from g-C3N4 to GO at the CG interface, which matches the result in Fig. 5A. Such a continuous secondary electron transfer mechanism base Z-scheme heterostructure would greatly improve the electron-hole pairs separation ability, thereby heighten the performance of the PEC.

Combined with the DFT results, the possible charge transfer process of the CGA modified electrode under irradiation has been depicted in Fig. 5C. For this Z-scheme heterostructure, the photogenerated electrons of g-C3N4 and Ag2CrO4 can be excited and transfer from the VB to the CB under light irradiation. Because Fermi energy must be identical at thermal equilibrium, the bands of g-C3N4 bend up due to its higher Fermi level, while the bands of Ag2CrO4 bend down on the contrary. The resulting IEF drives photogenerated electrons in the CB of Ag2CrO4 flow to the VB of g-C3N4. Moreover, the potential of GO (−0.49 eV) is lower than that of g-C3N4 (Xu et al., 2018a), thus it is thermodynamically beneficial for the transfer of photogenerated electrons from the CB of g-C3N4 to GO. Then part of electrons rapidly migrate from GO to the FTO electrode. Finally, the holes retain in the VB of Ag2CrO4 would oxidize OH− or H2O become O
H. The other part electrons in the GO can be captured by O2 to produce
O2 −.As a result, the CAP molecules could be oxidized directly by photogenerated holes in the VB of Ag2CrO4,
OH or O
2 −. The designed nanocomposite can accelerate the charge transfer and improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons-holes, producing a dramatically enhanced PEC signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the aptasensor in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To check the applicability of the proposed PEC method, the real samples of the pig urine and river water were chosen for assaying the CAP concentration, repeating 5 times for each sample. The result (Table. S3) showed that the recovery rate of the PEC aptasensor varied from 97.40 to 102.08 %. Besides, the recovery of CAP determined by the conventional HPLC method ranged between 98.20 and 100.88 %. The consistence of results above confirms the feasibility of the proposed method for CAP detection in the real sample.

In this work, an ultrasensitive PEC aptasensing method of CAP was established based on CAG composite material. A mass of experimental results have proved that the CAG heterostructure would play an important role in the field of PEC sensing. Moreover, the electron transfer pathway in Z-scheme heterostructure was proved by DFT calculation, which notably enhanced the photocurrent response. Due to the specific identification of aptamer and the Z-scheme CAG photoactive material, the designed PEC aptasensor possess the advantages of a wide linear range, a low detection limit as well as good selectivity, high reproducibility and ideal stability. In addition, the practicability of the fabricated sensor was successfully proved by detecting CAP in real pig urine and river water samples. The combination of theory and practice made the sensing principle more convincing, while the use of the proposed Z-scheme material provided a new direction for CAP detection.

Bo Peng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Yue Lu: Writing - review & editing. Jun Luo: Software, Writing - review & editing, Data curation. Ziling Zhang: Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Xu Zhu: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Lin Tang: Resources, Data curation, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Lingling Wang: Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Yaocheng Deng: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Xilian Ouyang: Formal analysis. Jisui Tan: Formal analysis. Jiajia Wang: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (numbers 51579096, 51521006 and 51409024); the National Innovative Talent Promotion Program of China (2017RA2088), the Funds for Innovative Province Construction of Hunan Province of China (2019RS3012). The authors would like to thank the support from Hunan Province Cooperative Innovation Center for The Construction & Development of Dongting Lake Ecological Economic Zone.

ce:section.ce:para: This file contains additional analytical methods Figs. S1–S4 and Tables S1–S3.
